questions.question: What happens when a subclass has a method with the same name, signature and access modifier as a method in its superclass?
options: The subclass method hides the superclass method
The superclass method hides the subclass method
No effect
The subclass method is not accessible
questions.explanation: In method overriding, the subclass method hides the superclass method. The subclass method is called instead of the superclass method when the class is instantiated.

questions.question: Why is dynamic method dispatch necessary in object-oriented programming?
options: Because it allows for multiple inheritance
Because it allows for method overriding
Because it allows for more flexibility and polymorphism
Because it is not needed
questions.explanation: Dynamic method dispatch is necessary because it allows for polymorphism, which is the ability of an object to take on multiple forms. This is necessary for method overriding and multiple inheritance.

questions.question: What is the purpose of using abstract classes in object-oriented programming?
options: To create concrete classes
To create abstract classes
To create interfaces
To create base classes
questions.explanation: Abstract classes are used to create base classes that provide a partial implementation of a class, allowing subclasses to extend and complete the implementation.

questions.question: Why is it a good practice to use the final keyword with inheritance?
options: Because it prevents subclassing
Because it prevents method overriding
Because it prevents multiple inheritance
Because it makes the class more secure
questions.explanation: Using the final keyword with inheritance prevents subclassing, which can make the code more predictable and easier to maintain.

questions.question: What is local variable type inference in Java?
options: A feature that allows for multiple inheritance
A feature that allows for method overriding
A feature that allows the compiler to infer the type of a local variable
A feature that prevents method overriding
questions.explanation: Local variable type inference is a feature in Java that allows the compiler to infer the type of a local variable, making the code more concise and easier to read.
